pages_preview.text_preview: Participating Laboratories Primary Manual for ManyBabies 3: A multi-lab study of infant algebraic rule learning Thank you for contributing to ManyBabies3, a project of ManyBabies, a cross-lab effort to provide an empirical basis for discussions of replicability as well as cultural, developmental, and methodological variability in infant perception/cognition research. In this project, we are examining babies’ ability to learn and generalize algebraic rules. This is following work from Marcus et al (1999), where infants were familiarized with 3-syllable sequences and then tested on new sequences that either follow, or don’t follow the familiarized pattern. More details about the background, design and hypotheses can be found in the Registered Report. Below we provide instructions on how to implement the experiment in your lab and report data back to the project as a whole. To-do summary 1. Complete the Initial Sign-Up Form. 2. Read this manual start to finish. 3. Before you begin data collection, please ensure that you have carefully read all of the documentation from the ManyBabies General Manual regarding authorship and ethics as well as the MB3 Collaboration Agreement. 4. Before you begin data collection, set up your study in consultation with this document. Carefully record any needed deviations from the protocol. Decide on your planned sample size/stopping rule. 5. Before you begin data collection, complete the Laboratory Questionnaire, submit Ethics approval and other documentation/materials. 6. Run pilot sample through data validator. 7. Send email to Melanie Soderstrom (M_Soderstrom@umanitoba.ca) to let the leadership team know that you are ready for ‘greenlighting’. 8. Wait for your official “green light” from the leadership team to begin data collection. 9. Collect your data! 10. Complete your participant and trial data files in consultation with the data reporting instructions. 11. Submit your data using the data upload form and complete the post data submission questionnaire and fill out the Credit Report Form. IMPORTANT: DO NOT begin data collection (other than piloting) until you have been explicitly (and individually) “green-lighted” to do so. 1

Bookmark not defined. Video records of data collection (optional) 22 Data dictionaries 22 Data Dictionary - Participant Columns 23 2

Getting started Start and End Date Data collection officially started January 1, 2023 and will initially run until December 31, 2023. Labs may join the ManyBabies 3 project anytime during the data collection period, provided that set-up and a formal “green light” is obtained, and data collection can be completed before the end date. However, we understand that there may be disruptions to data collection due to the current global situation related to the coronavirus outbreak. If you are having trouble meeting this timeframe, please alert the leadership team to discuss possibilities. Ethics approval It is a good idea to get started on your ethics approvals as soon as possible. Approvals must be in place and a copy submitted to the Drive folder (see below) before you can obtain a green light to collect data. Contact Melanie Soderstrom (M_Soderstrom@umanitoba.ca) if you need advice on obtaining ethics approval. Please make sure you have permission to publicly share anonymized raw data. ManyBabies 3 and other ManyBabies projects ask labs to collect a number of demographic background variables to be made public along with the main data, e.g. parental education, sex, et cetera. Not all of those variables are part of the main analysis. Even so, it is highly relevant to collect them for follow-up and secondary analyses, and for checking the representativeness of the sample. Please contact Melanie Soderstrom (M_Soderstrom@umanitoba.ca) if your ethics board raises concerns about collecting these data. We strongly encourage labs (where possible) to store/share video recordings of their testing on Databrary, which is a secure site for this purpose. You will need to ensure that you have ethics approval in place to do this, and collect consent from your participants. In addition, you will become a member of Databrary, which will require approval from your institution, so it’s helpful to start this process early. You can begin the registration process here. We are aware that many laboratories, particularly in the European Union, may not be allowed to use Databrary. In these circumstances we encourage labs to use alternative methods for sharing their videos where possible. Upload a copy of your Ethics approval documentation: Ethics documentation should be submitted prior to data collection. Ethics forms should be deposited in the Ethics Forms folder. Please ensure that all materials uploaded for ethics are clearly labeled in the filename with the lab ID you identified for your lab in the laboratory questionnaire (e.g. bllumanitoba_ethics.pdf). Check here for a complete listing of lab IDs to confirm yours. 4

Participants and Recruitment Age and numbers ● The minimum expected contribution is at least 16 babies (who meet eligibility criteria and have useable test trials (at least 1 usable ABA and one usable ABB test trial). Labs may review their data for the purpose of confirming that a participant has the two useable trials. See below “Eligibility and Exclusions”) between 5;0 and 12;0 months old (152-365 days). o Try to distribute participant ages across the full range of ages if possible ▪ Please report the exact age in days. You can use an online tool such as: https://www.calculator.net/age-calculator.html o In situations where labs planned but were unable to collect the minimum contribution, laboratory members will still be eligible for authorship. Alternative contributions may be requested by the Leadership Team in these circumstances. ● While we encourage as much participation as you can spare, it is crucial to the success of the project that you treat our study with the same care as you would any other study in your lab with respect to recruitment procedures, timing of data collection, and RAs/RA training. Please do not commit to providing data to ManyBabies if this level of care is not possible. Eligibility and Exclusions Please determine participants’ eligibility on the phone prior to scheduling them in the ManyBabies study to avoid testing ineligible participants. However, participants who come into the lab who are subsequently determined to be ineligible should be reported in the sample. ● Full-term. We define full-term as 37 weeks or more gestation. See demographic questionnaire for how this is calculated. ● Typical Development. No diagnosed developmental disorders (medical syndromes) that would plausibly impair performance in the task (e.g., neurocognitive or genetic disorders). ○ A physical development disorder would not be an exclusion criterion unless it prevents appropriate responding during testing (e.g. difficulty turning the head, significant visual impairment). ○ Developmental disorders will typically be self-reported in response to the question “Do you have any concerns about your child's development?” or a comparable expression typically used in your lab. “At risk” due to the presence of a condition in a sibling (without further reasons for concern) would not typically on its own be cause for exclusion. ● Normal hearing: Parent-reported (or formally diagnosed) hearing impairments are an exclusion criterion. ○ A history of ear infections is NOT on its own a criterion for exclusion 5

First-session/second-session policy Some laboratories have the practice of testing babies in more than one study during the same visit. ‘First session’ refers to babies tested soon upon arrival to the lab, prior to participating in any other studies. ‘Second session’ refers to any testing that is done after the first session. ⇒ Please contribute ‘first session’ babies when possible. It is possible that second session babies will contribute worse/weaker/different data with respect to the larger goals of determining effect sizes. ⇒ Please label ‘second session’ babies appropriately if infants were run in a different study on the same visit prior to their participation in MB3. Please also document the nature of the study run prior to MB3 for all ‘second session’ participants (e.g., “7 minute study of object categorization using eye-tracking”). Setting up the experiment Experiment Overview Each baby will participate in a 2-minute familiarization phase, followed by up to 12 15-second (max) test trials. During familiarization, a stream of 16 unique three-syllable sequences arranged in either ABB or ABA patterns will be presented. The test phase will consist of three blocks of four test items (two novel ABA and two novel ABB syllable sequences, order randomized within blocks), for a total of 12 test trials composed of syllables that are not presented in the familiarization phase. Equipment We assume you will use one of three procedures to run this study: (1) Headturn Preference (using Matlab, Habit, WISP, PsyScope, E-Prime or custom software) (2) Visual Fixation, single-screen (using Matlab, Habit, E-Prime, or custom software) (3) Eye-tracker (Tobii, EyeLink, SMI, ASL, or a different company, with appropriate software from the eye-tracker or custom software) We also assume you know how to set up looking-time studies on your equipment. If that is not the case, or if you need help, support is available from the following people: Headturn Preference, using PsyScope: [volunteer needed] Headturn Preference, using WISP: Casey Lew-Williams (caseylw@princeton.edu) Headturn Preference, using E-prime: Andreea Geambasu (a.geambasu@hum.leidenuniv.nl) Single screen, using Habit: Erin Hannon (erin.hannon@unlv.edu) (see set-up instructions here) Single screen, using E-prime: Andreea Geambasu (a.geambasu@hum.leidenuniv.nl) Single screen, using PsychoPy: [volunteer needed] 6
